result:
The implementation is correct — the single "failure" was a bug in my own test expectation, not the code: I asserted `SplitRow("\"\"\"\"")` should give `""` (two quotes), but the line `""""` is `JoinRow(["\""])` — a field containing one quote (wrapped, with the inner quote doubled) — and my implementation correctly returns `"`. All spec-derived cases and 20,000 randomized `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields))` round-trips over comma/quote/CR/LF-laden fields passed.

## Summary

The task was implemented in `CsvRow.calr`, the Calor-language source for this benchmark arm. Since Calor is a custom DSL, I first learned its idioms from the repo's syntax reference and from *other* benchmark pairs' reference files (I found this task's own answer key at `bench/.../N1-003-csv-row/reference/` and deliberately did not read it, so the solution is independent work).

The three new functions, following the fixture's existing conventions (`§F{fNNN:Name:pub} (args) -> type` signatures, `§E{}` purity annotations, `§C{...}§A...§/C` calls):

- **`EscapeField`** (`f002`) — returns the field verbatim unless `NeedsQuoting` holds, in which case it doubles internal quotes via `Replace` and wraps the result in quotes.
- **`JoinRow`** (`f003`) — folds over the list with a first-element flag, comma-separating `EscapeField` of each; the empty list yields `""`.
- **`SplitRow`** (`f004`) — a single-pass scanner with an `inQuotes` flag: outside quotes, a comma ends the field and a quote enters quoted mode; inside quotes, a doubled quote emits one literal quote and a lone quote exits; a final push emits the last field, which also makes the empty line parse to one empty field and a trailing comma produce a trailing empty field. It declares `§E{mut}` because the compiler's effect-enforcement pass (pinned ON in the csproj) infers the `mut` effect for `§PUSH` list appends.

`dotnet build` succeeds with 0 warnings and 0 errors (1 of the 10 allowed cycles used), and I verified behavior by compiling the generated C# in a throwaway scratch project — spec edge cases plus the randomized round-trip property all pass.
